coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and characterization of upconversion N-doped graphene quantum dots for improving photoelectrocatalytic performance of rutile hierarchical TiO₂ nanowires under visible and near-infrared light irradiations
coredata.dc:creator: Saeidi, S.
Rezaei, B.
Ensafi, A.A.
coredata.dc:description: The development of well-organized and low-priced photoelectrocatalysts for the clean and efficient water splitting reaction is crucial. In this context, novel nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with high photoluminescence and upconversion emission have been synthesized as excellent light harvester. Subsequently, ordered hierarchical TiO₂ nanowires were decorated with upconversion N-GQDs as a photoanode by a simple preparation method to improve the photocatalytic performance in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions of solar light, not otherwise absorbable by bare TiO₂ nanostructures. Moreover, the enhancement of charge transfer efficiency and electron–hole separation according to the energy states of N-GQDs and TiO₂ are considered for the improved photocatalytic performance of water splitting. N-GQDs/TiO2 shows superior photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) performance, achieving a photocurrent density of 3.0 mA.cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH solution, which is eight times that of unmodified TiO₂ at an applied voltage of 1.23 V vs. RHE. The high stability and photoelectrocatalytic activity of oxygen evolution reaction in the presence of newly synthesized N-GQDs are confirmed by chronoamperometry, open-circuit potential measurement, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The as-fabricated photoanode provides an increased solar light harvesting from UV–Vis to NIR due to the application of newly synthesized upconversion GQDs, which increase energy conversion with an appealing perspective.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene dots
Upconversion QDs
TiO₂ nanowire arrays
Photoelectrochemistry
Water splitting
Photoanode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of hierarchical TiO₂ nanowire arrays
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the synthesis procedure of N-CQDs, (b) TEM image of N-GQDs (the inset shows DLS result of N-GQDs), (c and d) FE-SEM images of hierarchical TiO₂ nanostructures in different scales, and (e) TiO₂ nanorods.
Elemental mapping of N-GQDs/TiO₂: (a) Ti (the inset shows EDS result), (b) O, (c) C, and (d) N.
UV–Vis spectra and Tauc plots of (a) N-GQDs and (b) TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra obtained from nitrogen-doped N-GQDs (the inset shows the intensity of fluorescent emission), (b) upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) of N-GQDs in the presence of cutoff filter (the inset shows the intensity of upconversion emission), (c) photoluminescence excitation spectra from N-GQDs, (d) normalized PL intensity for N-GQDs solution over time under Xe-lump irradiation, (e & f) The temperature and power density dependence of PL and UCPL integrated density excited by 360 nm in the absence and 680 nm in the presence of the cutoff filter.
(a) Fourier transform-infrared spectra of citric acid quantum dots and N-GQDs, (b) Raman spectra of N-GQDs, TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂.
X-ray diffraction patterns of N-GQDs, TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂.

PEC measurements: J-V curves of different photoelectrodes to study (a) temperature effect and (b) time effect in N-GQDs deposition process, (c) J-V curve of N-GQDs/TiO₂ photoelectrode prepared at optimum condition (300 °C and 30 min) in the absence and presence of light source illumination, (d) transient photocurrent response using an alternating light source with 30 s on/off cycles and voltage of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl.

(a) Nyquist plots in the dark and under illumination at 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl, (b) open-circuit potential measurement, (c) Mott–Schottky plots of TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂ photoelectrodes recorded in the dark and frequency of 3 kHz, and (d) The illustration of a purposed model for enhanced catalytic activity of N-GQDs/TiO2.
Comparing PEC efficiencies of 1D TiO₂ photoelectrodes with different modifications at 1.23 V vs. RHE.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and characterization of upconversion N-doped graphene quantum dots for improving photoelectrocatalytic performance of rutile hierarchical TiO₂ nanowires under visible and near-infrared light irradiations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of well-organized and low-priced photoelectrocatalysts for the clean and efficient water splitting reaction is crucial. In this context, novel nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with high photoluminescence and upconversion emission have been synthesized as excellent light harvester. Subsequently, ordered hierarchical TiO₂ nanowires were decorated with upconversion N-GQDs as a photoanode by a simple preparation method to improve the photocatalytic performance in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions of solar light, not otherwise absorbable by bare TiO₂ nanostructures. Moreover, the enhancement of charge transfer efficiency and electron–hole separation according to the energy states of N-GQDs and TiO₂ are considered for the improved photocatalytic performance of water splitting. N-GQDs/TiO2 shows superior photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) performance, achieving a photocurrent density of 3.0 mA.cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH solution, which is eight times that of unmodified TiO₂ at an applied voltage of 1.23 V vs. RHE. The high stability and photoelectrocatalytic activity of oxygen evolution reaction in the presence of newly synthesized N-GQDs are confirmed by chronoamperometry, open-circuit potential measurement, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The as-fabricated photoanode provides an increased solar light harvesting from UV–Vis to NIR due to the application of newly synthesized upconversion GQDs, which increase energy conversion with an appealing perspective.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the development of energy resources, there is still an urgent need to find affordable, high-efficiency, and inexpensive approaches [1]. Hydrogen is known as an environmentally friendly and high-calorific-value fuel, which can be obtained from water [2,3]. Although the main method for the formation of hydrogen molecules is water electrolysis, the combination of electrolysis and sunlight as two main accessible sources is a novel and remarkable approach [4,5]. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting based on semiconductors has been developed to store solar energy and improve the efficiency of hydrogen production [6–8]. Oxygen evolution reaction (OER), as a half part of the water splitting process with complicated electron transfer kinetics, has attracted attention for developing efficient photoelectrocatalysts [9]. In this regard, TiO₂ was introduced as a superior, abundant, non-toxic, and low-cost photocatalyst with proper band edge states by Hashimoto et al., in 2005 [10,11]. Despite these advantages, low photoexcitation toward visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions of the solar spectrum and inefficient charge separation are two challenging drawbacks causing the low photocatalytic performance of unmodified TiO₂ [12–14]. The enhancement and optimization of the performance of TiO₂-based photocatalysts in water splitting have been performed by some methods including verification of TiO₂ morphology, modification by heterojunctions, metal oxide compositions, and coupling with semiconductor quantum dots [15,16].

The arrangement of TiO₂ to 1D nanostructures including nanotubes, nanorods, and nanowires considerably improves its photoelectrochemical performance due to the enhanced surface area, reduced reflection light loss, and increased light absorption efficiency [17]. Moreover, these 1D structures can offer a straight path with a reduced number of grain boundaries to transfer excitons, which improve electron–hole diffusion and charge separation [11,18]. Further improvement in the photo-sensitivity of semiconductors is desirable by designing 3D nanostructures. Nanobranches and nanoflowers with higher surface area harvest sunlight and prepare many more active sites for electrochemical reactions and increased photon energy conversion [19,20].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with chemical, optical, and electronic advantages of both graphene structure and quantum dots show interfacial interaction with TiO₂ to affect charge carrier production and transfer efficiency [21,22]. The interaction of TiO₂ nanostructures decorated with GQDs, as sensitizer, broadens the absorption range, increases the charge transfer efficiency, reduces the photoinduced electron–hole recombination, and decreases the response time [23]. Doping of GQDs with interstitial atoms including N, S, P, Si, and B has already been shown to improve the absorption and emission efficiency and photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) [24]. At this point, nitrogen functionalized GQDs (N-QGDs) with outstanding features such as broad absorption band, large number of energy states, high PL QY, and photostability have been widely used [25,26].

Recent trends in energy resources have focused on compounds with special characteristics and new applications. The PL upconversion effect, which is referred to the emission of higher-energy photons on absorption of higher wavelength photons, has attracted the attention of researchers [27,28]. The upconversion phenomenon is described in two ways. The first one is the absorption of two or more low-energy photons and emission of a single photon with higher energy, and the second one is an anti-Stokes transition [29,30]. Some N-GQDs as particular candidates for photovoltaic devices and sensors exhibit upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties to make full use of solar spectrum [23].

An attempt has been made to obtain N-GQDs with a broad absorption range from UV to NIR, effective light sensitivity, and upconversion PL, from abundant and low-cost precursors to boost the PEC activity of N-GQDs/TiO₂ photoanode. The N-GQDs modified hierarchical rutile TiO₂ nanowire (HNW) arrays display advanced PEC performance in water splitting under NIR and visible regions of sunlight. In this study, florin-doped tin oxide (FTO) sheets were covered with HNWs vertically grown on the surface using a simple and efficient hydrothermal method. The enhanced photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) performance of TiO₂ in the presence of upconversion N-GQDs was evaluated in the absence and presence of N-GQDs. Newly synthesized upconversion N-GQDs with a high QY of 51.5% in combination with TiO₂ nanoarrays show enhanced photocurrent density of 3.0 mA.cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which confirms their capability in solar energy generation and water splitting.

Analytical grade citric acid (CA), tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA), and titanium isopropoxide (TTIP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) with the purity of 36.5–38.0% and potassium hydroxide (KOH, 99%) were purchased from Merck Chemical Co. DI-water was used for the synthesis through the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hierarchical TiO₂ nanowire arrays
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of TiO₂ nanowires on the surface of FTO glasses was performed by hydrothermal method as follows. A mixture of 15 mL of HCl, 15 mL of DI-water, and 0.5 mL of TTIP was magnetically stirred for 10 min under ambient conditions. Four pieces of FTO glasses with a surface area of 1.0 × 1.5 cm2 were then well cleaned in ethanol and acetone separately by ultrasonication and dried by a stream of N2 gas. FTO glasses were put into a 50-mL autoclave, and the prepared solution was transferred into the Teflon. The Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave was heated by an electronic oven under the optimized condition of 180 °C for 12 h. The prepared samples were washed with DI-water, dried at ambient pressure, and annealed in a furnace for 30 min at 500 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs were directly synthesized under solvent-free conditions using hydrothermal method. First, 2 mmol of CA (0.42 g) and 6 mmol of TEPA (1.134 g) were mixed for 30 min. The mixture was subsequently kept in an electric oven for 6 h at 180 °C. The color of the products then changed to dark brown. The synthesized N-GQDs were mixed with 15 mL of DI-water and centrifuged at 6000 rpm using an ultra-0.5 mL centrifugal filter unit.

Decoration of HNWs with N-GQDs was performed by dropping 70 μL of N-GQDs solution on the surface of TiO₂/FTO and spin coating at the rotation rate of 2000 rpm for 1 min. The as-prepared electrode was placed in a furnace under N2 gas flow for various temperatures and times to achieve the adhesion of N-GQDs. The temperature and time ranged from 220 to 350 °C and 30 min to 4 h, respectively, to obtain the best result.

The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of N-GQDs was recorded using a Philips CM30 300 microscope operating at 300 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and elemental mapping images were recorded using a quanta FEG 450 instrument. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was carried out using a Malvern ZEN3600 instrument to determine the size distribution of quantum dots. The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs were recorded on a JASCO FP-750 fluorophotometer. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using an AvaSpec-2048-USB2 spectrophotometer from 200 to 800 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a D8-Advance Bruker using Cu (Kα) emission operating at 35 mA and 40 kV in the 2θ range of 10° to 80°. Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded to determine functional groups on a Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer using KBr pellets.

A three-electrode cell configuration consisting of Ag/AgCl, a Pt wire and the prepared TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂ photocatalysts decorated on FTO as the reference, counter and working electrodes, respectively, to evaluate the photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) activity. Current density–voltage (J-V) response, transient photocurrent response (TPR), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), open-circuit potential (OCP), and Mott–Schottky analysis were performed under the illumination of a 35 W Xe-lamp with the intensity of 100 mW.cm−2 as a solar simulator (360 nm < λ < 1000 nm) in 1.0 M KOH solution. A self-made quartz photocell was used in all measurements.

Hydrothermal carbonization of CA and TEPA precursors at high temperature (180 °C) and pressure lead to an intermolecular condensation and dehydration to form carbon nuclei [31]. TEPA as the N source can easily introduce N atoms during the formation and growth of the graphitic structure to form N-GQDs (Fig. 1 a). The TEM image in Fig. 1b represents the shape and size distribution of N-GQDs prepared by the hydrothermal process. According to the DLS result, N-GQDs have a size distribution in the 5–12 nm range (Fig. 1b, inset image). The morphology and structure of grown TiO₂ on FTO surface and N-GQDs/TiO₂ were characterized by FE-SEM. FTO surface covered with TiO₂ was composed of ‘flower’ like HNWs (Fig. 1c and d), and in some parts packed TiO₂ nanorods are shown in the SEM image (Fig. 1e). Tetragonal nanorods grown on the surface of FTO have an average edge length of 60 nm. The grown hierarchical structures have an estimated diameter and length of 5 and 2.5 μm, respectively. EDS chemical mapping on N-GQDs/TiO₂ was carried out to obtain further information on the distribution of C, N, O, and Ti elements. Elemental mapping results (Fig. 2 c and d), indicate the uniform distribution of N-GQDs on the surface of HNWs, which results in photocatalytic activity improvement. EDS analysis results in Fig. 2a, inset image, show that the surface of the synthesized N-GQDs/TiO₂ photoelectrocatalyst is mostly covered by TiO₂ structures.

The UV−Vis absorption spectrum of synthesized N-GQDs dispersed in distilled water is depicted in Fig. 3 a. The two strong absorption bands around 245 and 360 nm and an extended tail continuing to near-IR (NIR) wavelengths are observed. Notably, π-π∗ transition is responsible for the strong absorption at 245 nm and n-π∗ transitions cause the next absorption peak at 360 nm. According to the Kubelka–Munk function, the Tauc plot of semiconductors was plotted to determine the band gap energies (Eg). Two bang gap energy values of 2.6 and 4.2 eV were calculated from the linear region of the Tauc plot for N-GQDs [32,33]. The optical absorption spectra of TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂ are compared in Fig. 3b. After the deposition of N-GQDs, the absorption intensity was noticeably enhanced from 500 to 800 nm, demonstrating the light-harvesting of the visible and NIR regions. In TiO₂ semiconductors with rutile phase, a bandgap with the energy of 3.0 eV is observed. The comparison of the Tauc plots of TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂ indicates the reduction of bandgap energy to lower values (2.6 eV) following the decoration of TiO₂ with N-GQDs, which is appropriate for photocatalytic OER.

The PL properties of the prepared N-GQDs were also studied. The synthesized N-GQDs were dispersed in distilled water and excited in the ranges of 300–420 nm and 660–740 nm without a filter and in the presence of a cutoff filter, respectively, to eliminate wavelengths lower than 400 nm and second-order diffraction of the monochromator. As shown in Fig. 4 a, by exciting the sample at 360 nm the maximum PL intensity appears at 445 nm. The UCPL intensity with excitation in the range of 660–740 nm shows maximum emission when excited with 680 nm centered at 470 nm (Fig. 4b). The obtained N-GQDs represents QY of 51.5%, indicating its high PL performance.

The results show the downconversion and UCPL characteristics of N-GQDs. To study the excitation process, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) tests were carried out in the absence (λem = 450 nm) and presence (λem = 460 nm) of cutoff filter to avoid interpretation of monochromator diffraction. According to the PLE results displayed in Fig. 4c, in the absence of the filter, the two clear excitation peaks at 250 and 360 nm are proper wavelengths for PL and in the presence of the filter and the excitation peak centered at 680 nm corresponds to the appropriate excitation wavelength of UCPL. The rest of the peaks are related to the Rayleigh scattering.

The photostability of as prepared N-GQDs has been studied by PL measurement of N-GQDs solution under continuous Xe-lump irradiation. Fig. 4d shows the PL emission intensity normalized to the value measured in the beginning. Data points show that during the first 30 min of irradiation, the PL emissions increased slightly. The surface rearrangement could be a reason for this enhancement [34,35]. After 8 h, the PL emission intensity remained high with approximately about 97% of its initial value before irradiation. The results demonstrate that the N-GQDs have acceptable photostability under the illumination.

To confirm the upconversion properties, multiple analyses were performed. The dependence of normal PL and UCPL on the temperature and power density of excitation source is depicted in Fig. 4e and f [36,37]. An upconversion process starts with the absorption of two or more photons in sequence. In some levels, phonon bath supply required energy and increased UCPL intensity. On the contrary, in normal PL, increasing temperature increases non-radiative transitions and decrease the emission intensity of PL. The results depicted in Fig. 4e confirm the different behaviors of normal PL and UCPL of N-GQDs. The integrated PL intensities show different slopes which account for the different behaviors of PL and UCPL processes in N-GQDs. The association between the integrated PL and excitation intensities is depicted in Fig. 4f. The linear relationship of normal PL to excitation intensity is related to only two transition states involved in the absorption process. On the other hand, in UCPL more than two excitation states have participated, which cause superliner dependence on the excitation density of the power source. As illustrated in Fig. 4f, normal PL and UCPL have linear and non-linear (2.303 order of excitation intensity) dependences, respectively. Accordingly, the experimental results confirm the UCPL of N-GQDs.

Future information on the functional groups in N-GQDs was obtained using FT-IR analysis and compared with FT-IR spectrum of QDs synthesized by CA precursor denoted as CA QDs. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 5 a), a broad absorption peak is observed at about 3000–3500 cm−1 related to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The presence of functional groups such as hydroxyl and amine bring about high hydrophilicity of the synthesized N-GQDs. Two bands at 2931 and 3066 cm−1 are ascribed to C–H (C–C–H) and C–H (C=C–H) with sp3 and sp2 structures, respectively. The stretching vibrational band at 1654 cm−1 is associated with –COOH group. The typical peaks at 1554 and 1436 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of C=C and C–N, respectively. The absence of N–H and C–N functional groups in the FT-IR spectrum of CA QDs imply the successful modification of GQDs with nitrogen atoms [38–40]. Raman spectroscopy was used as a non-destructive and effective method to characterize the N-GQDs and prepared electrocatalysts. Raman spectrum of N-GQDs is included in Fig. 5b. The peaks centered at 1386 and 1598 cm−1 are attributed to the D and G bands of carbon materials, respectively. The D and G bands are related to the sp3 defects in the disordered graphite and vibration of the in plane sp2 carbon atoms, respectively. The low intensity and broad D band are due to the incorporation of N into the graphitic structure of carbon atoms that somewhat disordered its structure. The formation of rutile TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂ is confirmed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 5b). The two obvious peaks of TiO₂ located at 450 and 613 cm−1 are assigned to Eg and A1g modes, respectively, related to the rutile phase of TiO₂ [41]. The presence of D and G bands, which are the Raman active modes of graphene in N-GQDs/TiO₂ Raman spectrum, show the successful combination of N-GQDs on HNWs [42–44].

The XRD patterns of N-GQDs, TiO₂, and modified electrodes (N-GQDs/TiO₂) represent their crystalline structures. The results are shown in Fig. 6 . The diffraction pattern of N-GQDs displays a peak at 22.5° corresponding to the graphite structure with [002] planes [42,45]. The as-prepared TiO₂ electrode exhibits major rutile phase with crystalline planes of [110], [101], [111], [210], [211], [220], [310], and [112] (TiO₂ rutile JCPDS card No. 21–1276) and diffraction peaks in 2θ of 25.57 [101] and 37.87 [004], which show the presence of anatase phase [46–48]. In the XRD pattern of N-GQDs/TiO₂ no diffraction peaks of the graphitic structure related to N-GQDs are observed. The graphitic structure may be distorted as a result of the small number of N-GQDs deposited on the surface of TiO₂ nanostructures. However, the proportion of anatase and rutile phase in N-GQDs/TiO₂ has been changed in comparison with TiO₂. The intensity of diffraction peaks related to the anatase crystalline structure decreased, while the intensity of crystalline planes of rutile phase increased. Rutile TiO2 is the most stable phase in high temperature and the second heat treatment step in the synthesis procedure of N-GQDs/TiO2 facilitate phase transformation from anatase to rutile with higher thermal stability [49–51].

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation using the as-prepared photoelectrocatalysts was performed in a standard three-electrode cell under Xe lamp with the illumination of 100 mW.cm−2. PEC measurements were performed vs. Ag/AgCl and Pt as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively, at a scan rate of 5 mV.s−1 in 1.0 M KOH solution as the electrolyte. The typical LSV plots of HNWs after decoration with N-GQDs at different pyrolysis times and temperatures were compared. The photocurrent of N-GQDs/TiO₂ photoanodes reaches its highest intensity of 3.0 mA.cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE by pyrolysis at 300 °C for 30 min as depicted in Fig. 7 a and b. The photocurrent density of N-GQDs/TiO₂ (0.37 mA.cm−2) is more than eightfold than that of the TiO₂ photoelectrode. Nevertheless, increasing the pyrolysis time from 30 min to 4 h resulted in a lower photocurrent, which could be due to the chemical changes in the N-GQD structure. The generation of electron–hole pairs in photoanodes increases with scanning the applied potential to positive values. After a sharp rise in the photocurrent, at the potential of 0.4 V vs. RHE, the photocurrent enhancement becomes slow, which shows that the charge recombination with counterpart holes hinder the photocurrent generation [52]. The LSV result of dark mode in Fig. 7c, shows a limited current density of ≈2 μA.cm−2, indicating that the applied potential is not able to supply the required overpotential for water oxidation. The photocurrent response of N-GQDs/TiO₂ reacts rapidly with no transition states upon irradiation by chopped light, which shows the fast charge transport and efficient separation of charge carriers in the photocatalyst [53]. The stability and TPR of N-GQDs/TiO₂ and TiO₂ photoanodes under alternative illuminations of the light source at 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl are depicted in Fig. 7d. Stable current density during 800 s is one of the strong points of both electrodes. The low photoresponse of TiO₂ in comparison with N-GQDs/TiO₂ indicates improved photocatalytic activity in the presence of N-GQDs. The results obtained were compared with those recently reported photocatalysts based on 1D TiO₂ semiconductor with different dopants. The comparison of the remarkable performance of N-GQDs/TiO₂ prepared by a simple preparation method using low-cost precursors with that of similar TiO₂-based photoanodes and high PEC is shown in Table 1 .

EIS measurements were carried out to understand more about the dynamics of electron transportation in the dark and light modes [60]. The semicircle diameter in Nyquist plots of TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂ associated with charge-transfer resistance of photoanodes are shown in Fig. 8 a. Lower semicircle diameter represents a faster charge-transfer mechanism. According to the results, the charge transfer resistance of N-GQDs/TiO₂ electrode under the illumination at 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl is significantly lower than that of TiO₂. The incorporation of N-GQDs into the electrode structure enhances electron transfer, reduces charge recombination, and finally improves the PEC process efficiency.

OCP analysis in which no charge transfer occurs, measures OCP values and their changes after polarization and activation of electrocatalyst or while the time passes, in contact with the electrolyte [61]. Thus, OCP measurement vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode was carried out in the dark and light modes over time, and the results are presented in Fig. 8b. The OCP instantly grows to higher potentials under light illumination, implying that the N-GQDs/TiO₂ photocatalyst has a good response to the generation of electrons [47]. The density of free electrons in the presence of illumination depends on electron–hole generation and separation. The generated photovoltage and transition states of N-GQDs/TiO₂ between dark and light modes are larger than those of pure TiO₂ and confirm its higher photoelectric conversion capability.

To better understand the electronic properties and improved electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/TiO₂, Mott–Schottky analysis was performed and the results are depicted in Fig. 8c. Mott–Schottky results give much information about the semiconductor–electrolyte interface. The relationship is defined as the following equation [62]:
1
C 2
=
2
ε
ε 0
A 2
e
N D
(
V −
V
f b
−
k B
T
e
)
where C is the interfacial capacitance, ε is the dielectric constant of rutile TiO₂ (ε = 170) [32], A is the surface area, and N
D is the charge carrier density, ε 0 is the permittivity of free space (8.854 × 10−14 F.cm−1), e is the electron charge (1.603 × 10−19 C), T is the operation temperature (298 K), k
B is the Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10−23 J.K−1), V is the applied potential to the electrode (vs. RHE) and V
fb is the flat band potential. The slope and intercept of Mott–Schottky plots conveniently determine N
D and V
fb of each semiconductor. The donor carrier concentrations of TiO₂ and N-GQDs/TiO₂ were calculated to be 9.16 × 1019 and 3.05 × 1020, respectively. A significant increase of donor density in the presence of N-GQDs and charge separation improves charge transport of N-GQDs/TiO₂, which results in enhanced photoelectrocatalytic performance.

According to the above results, a possible mechanism for enhanced photoelectrocatalytic properties of TiO2 in the presence of N-GQDs was discussed and is shown schematically in Fig. 8d. Under the illumination of the light source, UV radiations excite the large band gap TiO2 semiconductor to produce electrons (e−) and holes (h+). However, in the absence of the N-GQDs, charge recombination is the main process, which resulted in the low reactivity of TiO2 photoelectrode. In the presence of the N-GQDs, visible light irradiations are absorbed which exciting the electrons from HOMO level to LUMO level of N-GQDs. These photogenerated electrons transfer to the CB of TiO2 and finally transport to the counter electrode (Pt wire) to reduce H+ to H2 gas. Simultaneously, in photoanode, the holes in the HOMO level of the N-GQDs oxidize H2O molecules to O–H and the final product, O2 [63,64]. Moreover, low-energy photons that were absorbed with N-GQDs upconverted to the high-energy photons with proper energy to excite N-GQDs/TiO2 photoelectrode [37]. This structure noticeably reduces the charge recombination in TiO2 and improve the stabilization and charge separation in N-GQDs/TiO2 photoanode.

In summary, TiO₂ photocatalyst was fabricated by hydrothermal method and decorated by novel nitrogen-doped GQDs to form N-GQDs/TiO₂. Downconversion and UCPL of N-GQDs broaden the operational region of solar light for water splitting from UV to visible and NIR wavelengths. The photocatalytic activity and water oxidation of TiO₂ increased by more than eightfold in the presence of N-GQDs, as shown by the comparison of the electrodes before and after adding N-GQDs. Moreover, this improvement stems from the higher donor density than that of bare TiO₂, referring to Mott–Schottky results. The OCP values under illumination show the increased accumulation of photo-induced electrons and electron–hole separation in N-GQDs/TiO₂ photoelectrode. Such upconversion N-GQDs can be implemented as photosensitizers in photoelectrochemical devices. These results shed light on efficient energy conversion for commercial applications.

S. Saeidi: Conceptualization, Synthesis, Investigation, Characterization of compounds and Data analysis. B. Rezaei: Corresponding, Responsible for direction of experiment & Writing paper. A. A. Ensafi: Advisor and responsible for the direction of the experiment.
The funders had no role in the design of the study; in the collection, analyses, or interpretation of data; in the writing of the manuscript; or in the decision to publish the results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) for supporting this work.
